(**RE**), known until 1991 as the **Royal Society of Painter-Etchers and Engravers**, is a leading [art](/wiki/Art "Art") institution based in [London](/wiki/London "London"), [England](/wiki/England "England"). The Royal Society of Painter-Etchers, as it was originally styled, was a society of etchers established in London in 1880 and given a [Royal Charter](/wiki/Royal_Charter "Royal Charter") in 1888. Engraving was included within the scope of the Society from 1897, wood-engraving from 1920, coloured original prints from 1957, lithography from 1987 and all forms of creative forward-thinking original printmaking from 1990. The Society is in the process of refining its name to the Royal Society of Printmakers to both mirror and reflect the universality of original forms of creative expression within all forms, approaches and techniques across both traditional and contemporary printmaking.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Fellows_diploma%2C_Royal_Society_of_Painter-Etchers_and_Engravers.jpg/250px-Fellows_diploma%2C_Royal_Society_of_Painter-Etchers_and_Engravers.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fellows_diploma,_Royal_Society_of_Painter-Etchers_and_Engravers.jpg)

The fellows diploma of the Royal Society of Painter-Etchers and Engravers, produced by member [George W. Eve](/wiki/George_W._Eve "George W. Eve") in 1904.

The **Royal Society of Painter-Printmakers** (**RE**), known until 1991 as the **Royal Society of Painter-Etchers and Engravers**, is a leading [art](/wiki/Art "Art") institution based in [London](/wiki/London "London"), [England](/wiki/England "England"). The Royal Society of Painter-Etchers, as it was originally styled, was a society of etchers established in London in 1880 and given a [Royal Charter](/wiki/Royal_Charter "Royal Charter") in 1888. Engraving was included within the scope of the Society from 1897, wood-engraving from 1920, coloured original prints from 1957, lithography from 1987 and all forms of creative forward-thinking original printmaking from 1990. The Society is in the process of refining its name to the Royal Society of Printmakers to both mirror and reflect the universality of original forms of creative expression within all forms, approaches and techniques across both traditional and contemporary printmaking.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Society_of_Painter-Printmakers&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The Society was established on 31 July 1880 at 38 [Hertford Street](/wiki/Hertford_Street "Hertford Street"), Mayfair, London, as the Society of Painter-Etchers for the promotion of original etching as a creative art form.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The first six Fellows, all elected at this formation were [Francis Seymour Haden](/wiki/Francis_Seymour_Haden "Francis Seymour Haden") (English, 1818–1910); [Heywood Hardy](/wiki/Heywood_Hardy "Heywood Hardy") (English, 1852–1926); [Hubert von Herkomer](/wiki/Hubert_von_Herkomer "Hubert von Herkomer") RA (German/English, 1849–1914); [Alphonse Legros](/wiki/Alphonse_Legros "Alphonse Legros") (French, 1837–1911); [Robert Walker Macbeth](/wiki/Robert_Walker_Macbeth "Robert Walker Macbeth") RA, (Scottish, 1848–1910), and [James Tissot](/wiki/James_Tissot "James Tissot") (French, 1836–1902). [Samuel Palmer](/wiki/Samuel_Palmer "Samuel Palmer") (English, 1805–1881) – one of only two painter-etchers to be granted posthumous Honorary Fellowship of the RE – was terminally ill at the time of the Society's formation, otherwise would have been approached. [James McNeill Whistler](/wiki/James_McNeill_Whistler "James McNeill Whistler") (American, 1834–1903), who was in Venice at the time of the RE's founding, had a row with his brother-in-law, Haden, and was not invited to join. Letters in support were written to the fledgling Society by [Frederick Leighton](/wiki/Frederick_Leighton "Frederick Leighton"), then President of the [Royal Academy](/wiki/Royal_Academy "Royal Academy"), [John Ruskin](/wiki/John_Ruskin "John Ruskin"), [Charles Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens"), [John Everett Millais](/wiki/John_Everett_Millais "John Everett Millais"), and [Auguste Rodin](/wiki/Auguste_Rodin "Auguste Rodin"), amongst others. This Society achieved its [Royal Charter](/wiki/Royal_Charter "Royal Charter") granted by [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") in 1888 who bestowed on the President a gold chain of office, becoming the Royal Society of Painter-Etchers and in 1898 this was enlarged to include Engravers. Fellow, [George W. Eve](/wiki/George_W._Eve "George W. Eve"), designed a new Associates and Fellows diploma in 1893 and 1904.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

By 1911, when HM [King George V](/wiki/King_George_V "King George V") granted a Charter of Incorporation and Bye-laws, the RE, as it came to be styled, had grown in prestige and became fully established. From 1919, in token of solidarity, Presidents of the [Royal Academy](/wiki/Royal_Academy "Royal Academy") and the [Royal Watercolour Society](/wiki/Royal_Watercolour_Society "Royal Watercolour Society") (RWS) have been elected Honorary Fellows of the RE. The RE's original motto – "Never Stoop to be a Copyist" – changed to "[Nulla Dies Sine Linea](/wiki/Nulla_Dies_Sine_Linea "Nulla Dies Sine Linea")" (No Day Without A Line) in 1920.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Royal Society of Painter-Printmakers (formerly Royal Society of Painter-Etchers and Engravers) has had thirteen presidents (PRE) since 1880. They were: Sir Francis Seymour Haden (founder and PRE from 1880 to 1910), [Professor Sir Francis Job Short](/wiki/Francis_Job_Short "Francis Job Short") (PRE from 1910 to 1938), Professor [Malcolm Osborne](/wiki/Malcolm_Osborne "Malcolm Osborne") MBE (PRE from 1938 to 1962), Professor [Robert Austin](/wiki/Robert_Austin_(artist) "Robert Austin (artist)") (PRE from 1962 to 1970), [Paul Drury](/wiki/Paul_Drury "Paul Drury") (PRE from 1970 to 1975), [Harry Eccleston](/wiki/Harry_Eccleston "Harry Eccleston") OBE (PRE from 1975 to 1989), and [Joseph Winkelman](/w/index.php?title=Joseph_Winkelman&action=edit&redlink=1 "Joseph Winkelman (page does not exist)") (PRE from 1989 to 1991), when the Society was renamed.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Winkelman continued as president until 1995. Presidents elected by Members after this were Dr [David Carpanini](/wiki/David_Carpanini "David Carpanini") (PRE from 1995 to 2003), [Anita Klein](/wiki/Anita_Klein "Anita Klein") (PRE from 2003 to 2006), [Hilary Paynter](/wiki/Hilary_Paynter "Hilary Paynter") (PRE from 2006 to 2011), Dr [Bren Unwin](/w/index.php?title=Bren_Unwin&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bren Unwin (page does not exist)") (PRE from 2011 to 2013), [Mychael Barratt](/w/index.php?title=Mychael_Barratt&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mychael Barratt (page does not exist)") (PRE from 2013 to 2018).

Professor Dr [David Ferry](/w/index.php?title=David_Ferry_(artist)&action=edit&redlink=1 "David Ferry (artist) (page does not exist)") was elected President of the Royal Society of Painter-Printmakers in June 2018. Under Dr. Ferry’s acumen, energy and commitment, the RE’s prestige, profile and membership has expanded globally. To support the President and help direct the affairs of the Society there is an RE Council and four RE Officers as outlined in the 1888 RE Charter: these are Vice-president [Marianne Ferm](/w/index.php?title=Marianne_Ferm_(artist)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Marianne Ferm (artist) (page does not exist)") (VPRE from 2024), Hon. Treasurer Louise Hayward (since 2019), Hon. Curator (Historian & Archivist) Edward Twohig (since 2021) and current Hon. Secretary of the RE is Professor Timothy Emlyn Jones (since 2022).[[4]](#cite_note-4)

A strong affiliation with Guanlan International Print Biennial (China) and the Printmaking Museum of China has been made with the RE in recent years. Guanlan Original Printmaking Base covers all aspects of original printmaking: 'creation, production, display, collection, exchange, research, training and market development“.

## Membership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Society_of_Painter-Printmakers&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Membership")]

Membership, which was and still is restricted in number in order to make it a mark of distinction, is by election on the basis of work submitted to the Society's Council for peer review. In 1920 membership was expanded to allow artists who created prints from media other than metal, which allowed the election of woodcut artists such as [Gwen Raverat](/wiki/Gwen_Raverat "Gwen Raverat") and Noel Rooke. Another innovation in the same year was the formation of a [Print Collectors' Club](/w/index.php?title=Print_Collectors%27_Club&action=edit&redlink=1 "Print Collectors' Club (page does not exist)") to be limited to 300 members each of whom received a commissioned annual presentation print by members of the RE. Unusual in any Royal Society was that work by women and men was treated equally from the outset: election based on the quality of work regardless of gender and nationality.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Full Fellows are entitled to use the post-nominals RE. Associates, a class of members established in 1887, can use ARE. ARE's are elevated to RE full membership by majority vote election by the RE Council.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Notable members (and their date of election to a full fellowship) included: [Mary Nimmo Moran](/wiki/Mary_Nimmo_Moran "Mary Nimmo Moran") (1881[[5]](#cite_note-5)), [William Strang](/wiki/William_Strang "William Strang") (1881), [Joseph Pennell](/wiki/Joseph_Pennell "Joseph Pennell") (1882), [Auguste Rodin](/wiki/Auguste_Rodin "Auguste Rodin") (1882), [Charles William Sherborn](/wiki/Charles_William_Sherborn "Charles William Sherborn") (1884), [Herbert Dicksee](/wiki/Herbert_Dicksee "Herbert Dicksee") (1885), [Walter Sickert](/wiki/Walter_Sickert "Walter Sickert") (1887), [Sir David Young Cameron](/wiki/David_Young_Cameron "David Young Cameron") (1895), [Margaret Kemp-Welch](/wiki/Margaret_Kemp-Welch "Margaret Kemp-Welch") (1901), [Sir Frank Brangwyn](/wiki/Frank_Brangwyn "Frank Brangwyn") (1903), [Anna Airy](/wiki/Anna_Airy "Anna Airy") (1908), [Eugène Béjot](/wiki/Eug%C3%A8ne_B%C3%A9jot "Eugène Béjot") (1908), [Ernest Stephen Lumsden](/wiki/Ernest_Stephen_Lumsden "Ernest Stephen Lumsden") (1915), [William Walcot](/wiki/William_Walcot "William Walcot") (1920), [Edmund Blampied](/wiki/Edmund_Blampied "Edmund Blampied") (1921), [Gerald Brockhurst](/wiki/Gerald_Brockhurst "Gerald Brockhurst") (1921), [Robert Austin](/wiki/Robert_Austin_(artist) "Robert Austin (artist)") (1927), [Dame Laura Knight](/wiki/Laura_Knight "Laura Knight") (1932), [Sir William Russell Flint](/wiki/William_Russell_Flint "William Russell Flint") (1933), [Charles Tunnicliffe](/wiki/Charles_Tunnicliffe "Charles Tunnicliffe") (1934), [Geoffrey Wedgwood](/wiki/Geoffrey_Wedgwood "Geoffrey Wedgwood") (1934), [Joan Hassall](/wiki/Joan_Hassall "Joan Hassall") (1948), [James T.A. Osborne](/wiki/James_T.A._Osborne "James T.A. Osborne") (1957), [Gwenda Morgan](/wiki/Gwenda_Morgan "Gwenda Morgan") (1962), and [Robert Tavener](/wiki/Robert_Tavener "Robert Tavener") (1966). Others were elected as an Associate but did not achieve the full fellowship, such as [Eli Marsden Wilson](/wiki/Eli_Marsden_Wilson "Eli Marsden Wilson") (1907), [John Nicolson](/wiki/John_Nicolson_(artist) "John Nicolson (artist)") (1923) and [Salomon van Abbé](/wiki/Salomon_van_Abb%C3%A9 "Salomon van Abbé") (1923).

Since 1980 the Society is based at the [Bankside Gallery](/wiki/Bankside_Gallery "Bankside Gallery") in London.[[6]](#cite_note-6)
